modules.name: Regulations
modules.description: Covers the legal framework governing Part 107 operations — who can fly, how to register, what you can and cannot do, and what happens when things go wrong.

lessons.title: Eligibility & the Remote Pilot Certificate
lessons.intro: Before you can legally fly a drone commercially under Part 107, you need a Remote Pilot Certificate with a Small UAS Rating. This lesson covers who qualifies, how to get the certificate, and how to keep it current.

sections.heading: Who Is Eligible
sections.body: To be eligible for a Remote Pilot Certificate under §107.61, you must: (1) be at least 16 years old, (2) be able to read, speak, write, and understand English, (3) not know of any physical or mental condition that would make you unable to safely operate a small UAS. There is no requirement for an FAA medical certificate — you self-certify your fitness before every flight.
key_facts: Minimum age: 16 years old
English proficiency required (reading, speaking, writing, understanding)
No FAA medical certificate required — pilot self-certifies fitness
Must be in a proper physical and mental state to operate safely
sections.example: A 17-year-old who speaks English and passes the knowledge test can legally obtain and exercise a Remote Pilot Certificate.

sections.heading: How to Get Certified
sections.body: To obtain the certificate you must pass the FAA Aeronautical Knowledge Test (AKT) at an FAA-approved testing center. The test is 60 questions, you have 2 hours, and the passing score is 70% (42 of 60 correct). After passing, you apply through the FAA's IACRA system at iacra.faa.gov. You will need your FAA Tracking Number (FTN) — obtain this from IACRA before your test date.
key_facts: 60 questions, 2-hour time limit
Minimum passing score: 70% (42/60 correct)
Testing at FAA-approved testing centers
Apply via IACRA at iacra.faa.gov after passing
Bring your FTN (FAA Tracking Number) to the test
sections.memory_aid: 70% to fly — 42 right out of 60.

sections.heading: Keeping Your Certificate Current — Recency
sections.body: Your Remote Pilot Certificate does not expire, but your aeronautical knowledge recency does. Under §107.65, you must pass a recurrent knowledge test every 24 calendar months to remain current. If your knowledge currency lapses, you cannot legally act as remote PIC until you retake and pass the test. The recurrent test covers the same material as the initial test.
key_facts: Certificate does not expire
Knowledge recency expires every 24 calendar months
Must pass recurrent test to continue acting as remote PIC
Recurrent test covers same ACS topic areas as initial test
sections.example: You pass the initial test on March 15, 2024. Your knowledge is current until March 31, 2026 (end of that calendar month, 24 months later). After that date you must retake the test before flying.
sections.memory_aid: Certificate lasts forever. Knowledge lasts 24 months.

sections.heading: Supervising Non-Certificated Pilots
sections.body: A certificated remote PIC may allow another person — someone who does NOT hold a certificate — to physically manipulate the flight controls of the UAS, as long as the certificated remote PIC is present and maintains direct supervisory control. The certificated PIC retains full legal responsibility for the safe operation of the aircraft at all times. The non-certificated person does not need any certificate or rating to manipulate controls under this arrangement.
key_facts: Certificated remote PIC may supervise a non-certificated person manipulating the controls
Certificated PIC must be present and in direct supervisory control
Certificated PIC bears full legal responsibility
Non-certificated operator does not need their own certificate

common_mistakes: Confusing the 24-month knowledge recency with the certificate expiration (the certificate never expires)
Thinking you need an FAA medical certificate — you do not under Part 107
Forgetting to get your FTN from IACRA before the test day

lessons.title: UAS Registration
lessons.intro: Most drones must be registered with the FAA before flight. This lesson covers when registration is required, how to register, and how to display your registration number.

sections.heading: When Registration Is Required
sections.body: Under 14 CFR Part 48, all civilian small UAS weighing 0.55 pounds (250 grams) or more at takeoff — including everything onboard or attached — must be registered with the FAA before operation. This applies regardless of whether the flight is recreational or commercial. Aircraft under 0.55 lbs are exempt from Part 48 registration.
key_facts: Registration required at or above 0.55 lbs (250 grams) takeoff weight
Weight includes everything onboard or attached at takeoff
Applies to both recreational and commercial operations
Aircraft under 0.55 lbs are exempt
Part 107 aircraft over 55 lbs require Part 47 (paper-based) registration instead
sections.example: A DJI Mini 3 weighs 249 grams — under 0.55 lbs — so it is exempt from Part 48 registration. A DJI Mavic 3 weighs approximately 895 grams, so it must be registered.
sections.memory_aid: 0.55 to 55 lbs = Part 48 (online). 55 lbs and over = Part 47 (paper). Under 0.55 = no registration needed.

sections.heading: How to Display Your Registration Number
sections.body: Your registration number must be displayed on the aircraft in a location that is visible and accessible. The number must be legible and cannot be hidden inside a battery compartment that requires tools to open. You do not need to have your physical registration certificate with you during flight, but the number must be on the aircraft.
key_facts: Registration number must be visible and accessible on the aircraft
Must be legible
Cannot be hidden inside a tool-required compartment
One registration number can cover multiple aircraft owned by the same person (under Part 48)

summary_table.value: Visible and accessible on the aircraft

common_mistakes: Assuming only commercial flights require registration — recreational flights above 0.55 lbs also require it
Hiding the registration number inside the battery bay

lessons.title: Operating Rules — Limitations & Prohibited Acts
lessons.intro: Part 107's operating rules define what you can and cannot do in the air. These rules exist to protect other people in the sky and on the ground. Violations carry serious civil and criminal penalties.

sections.heading: The Hard Numbers — Operating Limits (§107.51)
sections.body: Every Part 107 pilot must know these four limits cold. They define the legal operational envelope for any flight without a waiver.
key_facts: Maximum altitude: 400 feet AGL (exception: within 400 ft horizontally of a structure, you may fly up to 400 ft above the structure's top)
Maximum groundspeed: 87 knots (100 mph)
Minimum flight visibility from control station: 3 statute miles
Cloud clearances: at least 500 feet below clouds AND 2,000 feet horizontally from clouds
sections.example: You are hired to inspect a 600-foot communications tower. You may fly up to 1,000 feet AGL (600 ft tower + 400 ft) as long as you stay within 400 feet horizontally of the structure. No waiver needed.
sections.memory_aid: 400 feet up. 100 mph forward. 3 miles to see. 500 below / 2,000 beside the clouds.

sections.heading: Daylight and Civil Twilight (§107.29)
sections.body: Standard Part 107 operations are restricted to daylight hours. Civil twilight — 30 minutes before official sunrise and 30 minutes after official sunset — is permitted but requires anti-collision lighting visible for at least 3 statute miles with a sufficient flash rate. True night operations (outside civil twilight) require a waiver. Alaska uses the definition of civil twilight from the Air Almanac, which differs from the lower-48 definition.
key_facts: Daylight operations: no special equipment required beyond standard kit
Civil twilight (30 min before sunrise / 30 min after sunset): anti-collision lighting required, visible 3 SM
Night operations (outside civil twilight): FAA waiver required
Lighting flash rate must be sufficient to avoid collision
sections.memory_aid: Civil twilight = 30 minutes either side of the sun. Lights on, visible 3 miles.

sections.heading: Right of Way (§107.37)
sections.body: A small UAS must yield the right of way to ALL other aircraft — manned or unmanned, large or small. This includes airplanes, helicopters, hot air balloons, gliders, and any other airborne vehicle. Yielding means the UAS must give way and may not pass over, under, or ahead of the other aircraft unless well clear. There are no exceptions. The UAS is always at the bottom of the right-of-way hierarchy.
key_facts: UAS must yield to ALL aircraft and airborne vehicles — no exceptions
Yielding means: give way AND do not pass over, under, or ahead unless well clear
This includes manned aircraft, other UAS, and launch/reentry vehicles
sections.example: You see a crop duster working in your area. You must maneuver your UAS to remain well clear and not interfere with its operations — even if you were there first.

sections.heading: Alcohol, Drugs, and Medical Fitness (§107.17, §107.27)
sections.body: No person may act as remote PIC, person manipulating the controls, or visual observer if they: (1) consumed any alcoholic beverage within the preceding 8 hours, (2) are under the influence of alcohol, (3) have a blood alcohol concentration of 0.04% or greater, or (4) are using a drug that affects their mental or physical capabilities. For medical conditions, pilots must self-assess — do not fly if you know of a condition that could interfere with safe operation.
key_facts: 8-hour 'bottle to throttle' rule — no alcohol within 8 hours
BAC limit: below 0.04%
Drug prohibition: anything affecting mental or physical capability
Applies to remote PIC, person manipulating controls, AND visual observer
No FAA medical required but pilot must self-assess fitness
sections.memory_aid: 8 hours, 0.04 BAC. Applies to the whole crew — not just the PIC.

sections.heading: Prohibited Acts
sections.body: The following are absolute prohibitions — no waiver exists for these: (1) Carrying hazardous material (§107.36). (2) Operating in a careless or reckless manner (§107.23). (3) Falsifying documents or applications (§107.5) — can result in certificate revocation. The prohibition on hazardous material is complete; it does not appear in the list of waivable rules in §107.205.
key_facts: Hazardous material carriage: NEVER permitted under Part 107
Reckless/careless operation: prohibited — can endanger life or property
Falsification: results in denial or revocation of any certificate held

summary_table.value: 400 ft above structure top, within 400 ft horizontally
30 min before sunrise to 30 min after sunset

common_mistakes: Thinking 400 ft AGL is always the maximum — the structure exception allows higher
Confusing civil twilight (no waiver needed, just lights) with night ops (waiver required)
Thinking the alcohol/drug rules only apply to the pilot manipulating the controls

lessons.title: Accident Reporting, Emergency Deviations & Waivers
lessons.intro: Knowing when to report, what to report, and who to report to is tested on every exam. This lesson also covers the in-flight emergency deviation rule and the waiver system.

sections.heading: FAA Accident Reporting (§107.9)
sections.body: The remote PIC must report to the FAA within 10 calendar days if an operation results in: (a) serious injury to any person or any loss of consciousness, OR (b) damage to any property other than the small UA, unless the cost of repair is $500 or less AND the fair market value of the property is $500 or less in the event of total loss. The report is made to the FAA — not the NTSB (unless separate NTSB criteria apply under 49 CFR Part 830).
key_facts: Report within 10 calendar days
Triggers: serious injury, loss of consciousness, or property damage over $500
No report required if property damage repair cost ≤ $500 AND fair market value ≤ $500
Report goes to the FAA
Separate NTSB reporting rules exist under 49 CFR Part 830
sections.example: Your drone clips a fence post and damages it. Repair cost is $350. No FAA report required. But if you hit a car and cause $1,200 in damage, you must report within 10 days.
sections.memory_aid: 10 days. $500 threshold. Injury or serious damage = always report.

sections.heading: In-Flight Emergency Deviation (§107.21)
sections.body: During an in-flight emergency, a remote PIC may deviate from ANY rule in Part 107 to the extent necessary to respond to the emergency. If a deviation occurs, the pilot must send a written report to the FAA only if asked to do so by the Administrator. This deviation authority is narrow — it is for genuine emergencies, not convenience. It is separate from the accident reporting requirement.
key_facts: May deviate from ANY Part 107 rule during a genuine emergency
Written report required ONLY if FAA Administrator requests it
This is NOT the same as the accident report — different trigger, different process
Must actually be an emergency — not a workaround for inconvenient rules
sections.example: Your drone has a battery fire mid-flight. You climb above 400 feet AGL to maneuver away from people. This is a legal deviation. You only need to report it if the FAA later asks you to.

sections.heading: The Waiver System (§107.200, §107.205)
sections.body: Some Part 107 restrictions can be waived by the FAA if the applicant can demonstrate the operation can be conducted safely. Waivers are obtained through the FAA DroneZone (formerly the FAA UAS portal). The regulations that CAN be waived include: daylight operations, VLOS, visual observer, multiple aircraft, right-of-way (§107.37a), over people, airspace authorization, and operating limits. Rules that CANNOT be waived include: the requirement for a remote pilot certificate, the prohibition on hazardous material, and the prohibition on falsification.
key_facts: Waivers obtained through FAA DroneZone
Can waive: night ops, VLOS, over people, multiple aircraft, airspace, and more
Cannot waive: pilot certificate requirement, hazardous material prohibition, falsification rules
Applicant must demonstrate the operation can be conducted safely
sections.memory_aid: You can waive most operational rules. You can NEVER waive the certificate requirement or hazmat prohibition.

summary_table.value: Any serious injury or loss of consciousness
Damage repair cost or FMV over $500

common_mistakes: Confusing the 10-day accident report (§107.9) with the emergency deviation report (§107.21)
Thinking any deviation from Part 107 triggers a mandatory report — only accidents do (or emergency deviations when FAA requests it)
Thinking all Part 107 rules can be waived — hazmat and certificate requirements cannot

modules.name: Airspace & Charts
modules.description: Covers the structure of U.S. airspace, how to read sectional charts, and how to identify what kind of authorization — if any — is needed to fly in a given location.

lessons.title: Controlled Airspace — Classes B, C, D, and E
lessons.intro: The U.S. airspace system is divided into classes — each with different rules and authorization requirements. As a remote pilot, you need to know which class you're operating in before every flight, because flying in controlled airspace without authorization is a federal violation.

sections.heading: The Big Picture — Controlled vs. Uncontrolled
sections.body: There are two main categories: controlled (Classes A through E) and uncontrolled (Class G). In controlled airspace, ATC provides some level of service and often requires authorization. In Class G, ATC has no jurisdiction and no authorization is needed. For UAS pilots, the key rule is §107.41: you need prior ATC authorization before flying in Class B, C, D, or Class E designated to the surface at an airport.
key_facts: Controlled: Classes A, B, C, D, E — ATC provides service
Uncontrolled: Class G — ATC has no jurisdiction
§107.41 requires prior ATC authorization for B, C, D, and surface-level E
Authorization obtained via LAANC or FAA DroneZone
sections.memory_aid: B, C, D, and E-at-surface = need authorization. Class G = go freely.

sections.heading: Class B — Busiest Airports
sections.body: Class B surrounds the nation's busiest airports, from the surface to approximately 10,000 feet MSL. The shape resembles an upside-down wedding cake — multiple layers of different radii. ATC authorization is required before entering. On sectional charts, Class B is depicted with solid blue lines.
key_facts: Surrounds busiest airports (major hubs)
Surface to approximately 10,000 ft MSL
Shape: upside-down wedding cake (layered rings)
Chart depiction: solid blue lines
Authorization required: yes (LAANC/DroneZone)
sections.memory_aid: B = Big, Blue, Busy. Solid blue lines on the chart.

sections.heading: Class C — Major Airports
sections.body: Class C surrounds airports with an operational control tower, radar approach control, and significant traffic. It typically has a 5 NM surface area (surface to 4,000 ft AGL above airport elevation) and a 10 NM outer shelf (1,200 to 4,000 ft AGL). ATC authorization required. Depicted on charts with solid magenta lines.
key_facts: Around airports with tower, radar approach, and significant operations
Surface to 4,000 ft AGL (charted MSL)
Inner ring: 5 NM radius; outer shelf: 10 NM radius
Chart depiction: solid magenta lines
Authorization required: yes
sections.memory_aid: C = Congested. Solid Magenta. 5 and 10 NM rings.

sections.heading: Class D — Towered Airports
sections.body: Class D surrounds airports with an operational control tower. It extends from the surface to 2,500 feet above airport elevation. The configuration is individually tailored. ATC authorization required. Depicted on charts with dashed blue lines.
key_facts: Surrounds airports with operating control tower only
Surface to approximately 2,500 ft above airport elevation (charted MSL)
Chart depiction: dashed blue lines
Authorization required: yes
sections.memory_aid: D = Dainty dashed blue. Smallest controlled ring.

sections.heading: Class E — The 'Everywhere Else' Controlled Airspace
sections.body: Class E is controlled airspace that isn't A, B, C, or D. It exists at multiple altitudes and is depicted differently depending on its floor. When Class E goes to the surface at an airport, ATC authorization is required for UAS — just like B, C, and D. When Class E starts at 700 ft or 1,200 ft AGL, no authorization is typically needed for UAS flying below that floor in Class G. Class E also covers all controlled airspace from 14,500 ft MSL to 18,000 ft MSL.
key_facts: Class E at the SURFACE: depicted with dashed magenta lines — authorization required
Class E starting at 700 ft AGL: depicted with magenta vignette/halo — no auth needed below 700 ft (you're in G)
Class E starting at 1,200 ft AGL: depicted with blue vignette/halo — no auth needed below 1,200 ft
Class E from 14,500 ft MSL to 18,000 ft MSL: exists everywhere else
sections.example: A rural airport has Class E airspace designated to the surface (dashed magenta circle). Even though there's no tower, you need LAANC authorization to fly your drone there. Five miles away with no surface designation, you're in Class G — no authorization needed.
sections.memory_aid: E is Everywhere. Solid line = surface level = authorization needed. Halo/vignette = starts above ground = may be free below it.

summary_table.value: Solid blue | Surface to ~10,000 MSL | Auth required
Solid magenta | Surface to 4,000 AGL | Auth required
Dashed blue | Surface to ~2,500 AGL | Auth required
Dashed magenta | Surface up | Auth required
Magenta vignette | 700 ft AGL up | No auth below 700 ft
Blue vignette | 1,200 ft AGL up | No auth below 1,200 ft
No depiction | Below Class E | No auth required

common_mistakes: Confusing dashed blue (Class D) with dashed magenta (Class E surface)
Assuming Class E starting at 700 ft means you need authorization below 700 ft — below 700 ft is Class G
Not realizing Class E can go to the surface at smaller airports that have no tower

lessons.title: Special Use Airspace & Other Airspace Areas
lessons.intro: Beyond the lettered classes, there is a category called Special Use Airspace (SUA) — areas where operations are restricted or hazardous. Remote pilots must recognize SUA on sectional charts and understand the rules for each type.

sections.heading: Prohibited Areas (P)
sections.body: Prohibited areas contain airspace where ALL aircraft flight is prohibited — no exceptions, no waivers. They are established for national security or national interest. Depicted on charts with a blue boundary and labeled P followed by a number (e.g., P-40 around Camp David).
key_facts: Flight of all aircraft PROHIBITED — no exceptions
Established for security or national interest
Chart label: P-### (e.g., P-40, P-56)
Examples: Camp David (P-40), National Mall in Washington D.C.

sections.heading: Restricted Areas (R)
sections.body: Restricted areas contain activities hazardous to non-participating aircraft (artillery firing, missile testing, aerial gunnery). Flight is NOT wholly prohibited but IS subject to restrictions. Entry without authorization from the using or controlling agency may be extremely hazardous. Depicted on charts labeled R followed by a number.
key_facts: Hazardous activities present — flight restricted but not fully prohibited
Must get authorization from the using or controlling agency
Chart label: R-### (e.g., R-4401)
Examples: military test ranges, artillery ranges

sections.heading: Warning Areas (W)
sections.body: Warning areas are similar to restricted areas but are located over international waters (3+ NM from the U.S. coast). The FAA cannot prohibit foreign aircraft from flying in international waters, so these areas carry a 'warning' rather than a 'restriction.' Depicted with W followed by a number.
key_facts: Similar to restricted areas but over international waters
Cannot be legally restricted — only warned
Chart label: W-###

sections.heading: Military Operations Areas (MOAs)
sections.body: MOAs separate military training from IFR traffic. VFR pilots (including UAS operators) may fly through MOAs but should exercise extreme caution — high-speed military aircraft may be present. Before flying through an MOA, check whether it is active. Depicted on charts with magenta boundaries and a label like 'VANCE 2 MOA.'
key_facts: Separates military training from IFR traffic
VFR/UAS flights permitted but exercise extreme caution
Check if active before flying through
High-speed military aircraft may be present below 10,000 ft MSL

sections.heading: Alert Areas (A)
sections.body: Alert areas inform non-participating pilots of areas with high volumes of pilot training or unusual aerial activity. No restriction applies — you can fly through, but both participating and transiting pilots share equal responsibility for collision avoidance. Depicted on charts labeled A followed by a number (e.g., A-211).
key_facts: High volume pilot training or unusual activity — awareness only
No prohibition or restriction
Equal collision avoidance responsibility for all pilots
Chart label: A-###

sections.heading: Controlled Firing Areas (CFAs)
sections.body: CFAs contain hazardous activities, but the key difference from restricted areas is that ALL activity is suspended when a spotter detects an approaching aircraft. Because CFAs do not cause non-participating aircraft to change course, they are NOT charted on sectional charts.
key_facts: Activities suspended when approaching aircraft is detected
Non-participating aircraft do not need to deviate
NOT charted on sectional charts — this is a key distinguishing fact
sections.memory_aid: CFAs are not on the chart because they always yield to you.

sections.heading: Military Training Routes (MTRs)
sections.body: MTRs are routes used by military aircraft for low-altitude, high-speed training, typically below 10,000 ft MSL at speeds over 250 knots. They are identified on sectional charts as IR (IFR) or VR (VFR) followed by a number. CRITICAL: A four-digit route number (e.g., VR1667) means ALL segments are at or below 1,500 ft AGL — military aircraft could be flying at drone altitudes on these routes. A three-digit route number (e.g., VR207) means at least one segment is above 1,500 ft AGL.
key_facts: IR routes: conducted under IFR regardless of weather
VR routes: conducted under VFR
FOUR digit number (e.g., VR1207): ALL segments at or below 1,500 ft AGL
THREE digit number (e.g., VR207): at least one segment above 1,500 ft AGL
Military aircraft may exceed 250 knots
sections.memory_aid: 4 digits = all segments BELOW 1,500 ft. Your drone at 400 ft is in their backyard.

sections.heading: Temporary Flight Restrictions (TFRs)
sections.body: TFRs temporarily restrict aircraft from operating in a defined area. They are issued as Flight Data Center (FDC) NOTAMs. Reasons include: disaster/hazard areas, firefighting operations, VIP movement (President, Vice President), national disasters, space operations, and major public events. Check tfr.faa.gov before every flight. Violating a TFR is a serious federal offense with major penalties.
key_facts: Issued as FDC NOTAMs
Check tfr.faa.gov before every flight
No altitude exemption — a TFR usually starts at the surface
Drone operators have grounded firefighting aircraft — a serious safety hazard
Violations result in certificate suspension, fines, and possible criminal prosecution
sections.memory_aid: TFR = Total Flight Restriction. No altitude workaround. Check tfr.faa.gov every time.

summary_table.value: No flight at all — no exceptions
Restricted — need authorization from controlling agency
Military training — VFR/UAS may enter but exercise caution
High traffic area — no restriction, shared responsibility
All segments ≤1,500 ft AGL — military at drone altitudes
Temporary restriction — check tfr.faa.gov, no-fly surface up

common_mistakes: Confusing Alert Areas (you can fly through) with Restricted Areas (you cannot without authorization)
Not checking for TFRs before flight — TFRs are not shown on sectional charts permanently, they change daily
Not knowing that 4-digit MTR routes have ALL segments at or below 1,500 ft AGL — the most dangerous for UAS

lessons.title: Reading Sectional Charts
lessons.intro: Sectional charts are the primary navigation and airspace reference for VFR pilots. The Part 107 exam includes chart-based questions where you must interpret symbols, locate airspace boundaries, and read obstacle heights. This lesson walks through the most tested symbols.

sections.heading: The Basics
sections.body: Sectional charts are published at a scale of 1:500,000 (1 inch = approximately 6.86 nautical miles or 8 statute miles). They are updated semiannually. The FAA Airman Knowledge Supplement (the booklet given to you at the testing center) contains sectional chart excerpts and a legend you can reference during the test. Use the legend — many chart questions can be answered directly from it.
key_facts: Scale: 1:500,000 (1 inch ≈ 6.86 NM or 8 SM)
Updated semiannually for contiguous U.S.
You receive a chart supplement booklet at the test — use it
Always refer to the legend for symbols you don't recognize

sections.heading: Obstacle Heights — MSL vs. AGL
sections.body: Obstacles on sectional charts (towers, antennas, wind turbines) display two numbers. The TOP number is the obstacle's height in feet MSL (Mean Sea Level). The BOTTOM number in parentheses is the height in feet AGL (Above Ground Level). The MSL number is always higher because it adds the ground elevation below. A UAS pilot needs to know the AGL height to avoid the obstacle since the 400 ft rule is AGL.
key_facts: Top number = MSL height
Bottom number (in parentheses) = AGL height
MSL is always the larger number
A lighted obstacle will have a lightning bolt symbol on the chart
sections.example: Chart shows: 1,549 / (312). The obstacle top is 1,549 ft MSL and 312 ft AGL. Since you're limited to 400 ft AGL, this obstacle is no conflict. If it showed 1,549 / (412), you'd have a problem.
sections.memory_aid: Top = MSL (the bigger number). Bottom = AGL (the one that matters for your 400 ft limit).

sections.heading: Airport Symbols
sections.body: Airports appear on sectional charts with different symbols based on type. A solid circle or elongated shape with runway depiction = public-use airport. An 'R' in a circle = private (restricted use) airport. Airports with control towers are depicted in blue; airports without towers are in magenta. The numbers near each airport give the field elevation in feet MSL and the traffic pattern altitude.
key_facts: Blue airport = has operating control tower
Magenta airport = no operating control tower
R in a circle = private airport
Numbers near airport = field elevation (MSL) and traffic pattern info
Runway lengths and available services also depicted

sections.heading: VFR Reporting Points, CTAF, and Frequencies
sections.body: A small magenta flag on a chart is a VFR waypoint/reporting point — manned aircraft use these to report position to ATC, indicating higher traffic volume in that area. To find the CTAF for a non-towered airport, locate the airport on the chart and find the 'C' symbol — the frequency listed is the CTAF. Most non-towered airports use UNICOM on 122.8 MHz, but always verify on the chart.
key_facts: Magenta flag = VFR reporting point = expect higher manned traffic
CTAF found on chart near the airport — look for the 'C' symbol
Default UNICOM: 122.8 MHz (always verify on chart)
MULTICOM (no UNICOM available): 122.9 MHz

sections.heading: Latitude, Longitude, and Navigating the Chart
sections.body: Latitude lines (parallels) run east-west and measure north-south position from the equator (0° to 90° N or S). Longitude lines (meridians) run north-south and measure east-west from the Prime Meridian through Greenwich, England (0° to 180° E or W). On sectional charts, each degree is divided into 60 minutes. One minute of latitude = 1 nautical mile.
key_facts: Latitude = north-south position, measured from equator
Longitude = east-west position, measured from Prime Meridian
1 minute of latitude = 1 nautical mile
60 minutes = 1 degree
U.S. contiguous states: approx. 25°-49° N lat, 67°-125° W lon
sections.memory_aid: Latitude = ladder rungs (horizontal lines climbing up). Longitude = long lines running pole to pole.

sections.heading: True vs. Magnetic North
sections.body: Sectional charts are oriented to TRUE north. However, runway headings, VOR compass roses, and PIREP bearings reference MAGNETIC north. The rule of thumb: 'If it is in print on a chart, it is true. If it is heard (ATC, radio), it is magnetic.' Isogonic lines on charts are broken magenta lines showing points of equal magnetic variation from true north.
key_facts: Chart is oriented to true north
Runway numbers are magnetic (rounded to nearest 10°, last digit dropped)
METAR wind direction = TRUE
ATC wind advisories = MAGNETIC
Isogonic lines = equal magnetic variation (broken magenta lines)
sections.memory_aid: In print = true. Heard = magnetic. Runway 27 = 270° magnetic heading.

summary_table.value: VFR reporting point — high manned traffic

common_mistakes: Confusing MSL (top, larger number) with AGL (bottom, smaller number) on obstacle depictions
Forgetting that runway headings are magnetic but METAR winds are true
Not using the chart supplement legend during the test — it's available to you

modules.name: Weather
modules.description: Covers how to read weather reports, interpret forecasts, and understand how atmospheric conditions affect UAS performance and safety.

lessons.title: METARs — Reading Current Weather
lessons.intro: The METAR (Meteorological Aerodrome Report) is the primary report of current surface weather conditions at an airport. The exam reliably includes questions where you must decode a METAR. This lesson walks through each element in sequence.

sections.heading: METAR Format — Element by Element
sections.body: Every routine METAR follows the same fixed sequence. Learn the order and you can decode any METAR. The elements are: Type → Station ID → Date/Time → Modifier → Wind → Visibility → Weather → Sky Condition → Temperature/Dew Point → Altimeter → Remarks.
key_facts: METAR = routine observation. SPECI = special (conditions changed rapidly)
Station ID: 4-letter ICAO code. In the contiguous U.S., begins with K (e.g., KGGG, KMDW)
Date/Time: 6 digits — first 2 = day of month, last 4 = UTC time with Z suffix
AUTO modifier: automated station (no human observer)
sections.example: METAR KGGG 161753Z AUTO — Routine METAR for Gregg County Airport on the 16th at 1753 Zulu, automated station.

sections.heading: Wind
sections.body: Wind is reported in the format DDDSSKT where DDD is the direction the wind is coming FROM in degrees TRUE, SS is the speed in knots, and KT ends the group. If gusting, the format adds G and peak gust (e.g., 14021G26KT = from 140° true at 21 knots gusting to 26 knots). Variable winds are reported as VRB.
key_facts: Direction is where the wind is coming FROM
Direction in TRUE degrees (not magnetic)
G followed by a number = gust speed
VRB = variable direction
sections.example: 14021G26KT = wind from 140° true at 21 knots, gusting to 26 knots.
sections.memory_aid: In print, it must be true. METAR wind = true north.

sections.heading: Visibility
sections.body: Visibility is reported in statute miles (SM). It may include fractions (e.g., 1 1/2SM). The part 107 minimum is 3 SM from the control station. If visibility is greater than 6 SM, METARs for many automated stations simply report P6SM (plus 6 statute miles).
key_facts: Reported in statute miles (SM)
Part 107 minimum visibility: 3 SM
P6SM = greater than 6 statute miles

sections.heading: Weather Phenomena
sections.body: Weather phenomena use a two-part code: intensity qualifier (+, –, or none for moderate) + descriptor + phenomenon. Common examples: RA = rain, SN = snow, TS = thunderstorm, BR = mist, FG = fog, HZ = haze. Intensity: – = light, no prefix = moderate, + = heavy. VC = in the vicinity (5–10 miles from airport).
key_facts: - (minus) = light intensity
No prefix = moderate
+ (plus) = heavy
TS = thunderstorm, RA = rain, SN = snow, FG = fog, BR = mist, HZ = haze
+TSRA = heavy thunderstorm with rain — a no-fly condition
sections.memory_aid: Plus is heavy. Minus is light. TS = thunderstorm.

sections.heading: Sky Condition
sections.body: Sky condition reports cloud coverage and height. Coverage is in eighths: FEW (1/8–2/8), SCT/Scattered (3/8–4/8), BKN/Broken (5/8–7/8), OVC/Overcast (8/8), SKC/Clear (0). Height follows as a 3-digit number representing hundreds of feet AGL — add two zeros. A ceiling is the lowest layer reported as BKN or OVC. CB = cumulonimbus (most dangerous cloud type).
key_facts: FEW = 1/8–2/8 sky coverage
SCT (Scattered) = 3/8–4/8
BKN (Broken) = 5/8–7/8 — constitutes a ceiling
OVC (Overcast) = 8/8 — constitutes a ceiling
Cloud height: 3-digit number × 100 = feet AGL (BKN008 = broken at 800 ft AGL)
CB = cumulonimbus — always note these
sections.example: OVC007 = overcast at 700 feet AGL. BKN008 OVC012CB = broken at 800 ft, overcast at 1,200 ft with cumulonimbus. The ceiling is at 800 ft.
sections.memory_aid: Three digits, add two zeros. BKN and OVC are ceilings. Higher number = higher altitude.

sections.heading: Temperature, Dew Point, and Altimeter
sections.body: Temperature and dew point are in degrees Celsius, separated by a slash (18/17 = 18°C temp, 17°C dew point). A small spread between temperature and dew point means high humidity and fog is possible. Temperatures below 0°C are prefixed with M (e.g., M05 = -5°C). Altimeter setting follows, preceded by A (e.g., A2970 = 29.70 in Hg).
key_facts: Temperature/dew point in degrees Celsius
M prefix = minus (below freezing)
Small temp-dew point spread = high relative humidity, fog possible
Altimeter prefixed by A, in inches of mercury (in Hg)
Rising pressure = fair weather. PRESFR in remarks = falling rapidly (watch out)

summary_table.value: True north, direction wind is FROM

common_mistakes: Reading BKN008 as 8,000 feet — it is 800 feet AGL
Thinking METAR winds are magnetic — they are true
Confusing the ceiling (BKN or OVC) with any cloud layer

lessons.title: TAFs and Other Weather Products
lessons.intro: While a METAR tells you what the weather IS right now, a TAF (Terminal Aerodrome Forecast) tells you what it is EXPECTED to be. Knowing how to read TAFs and other forecast products is essential for preflight planning.

sections.heading: Terminal Aerodrome Forecast (TAF)
sections.body: A TAF is a forecast for conditions within a 5 statute mile radius around an airport. It is valid for 24 or 30 hours and updated four times daily at 0000Z, 0600Z, 1200Z, and 1800Z. TAFs use the same abbreviations as METARs but include change groups to show how conditions will evolve.
key_facts: Forecast for 5 SM radius around the airport
Valid 24 or 30 hours
Updated 4 times daily (0000Z, 0600Z, 1200Z, 1800Z)
Uses same abbreviations as METAR

sections.heading: TAF Change Groups
sections.body: TAFs use specific codes to indicate expected changes. FM (From) = rapid, significant change expected within approximately one hour, followed by time in UTC. TEMPO = temporary fluctuations expected to last less than 1 hour and occur less than half the time period. BECMG (Becoming) = gradual change expected over a 2-hour period. PROB30 = 30% probability of conditions occurring — not used in the first 6 hours of a forecast.
key_facts: FM = From (rapid change): FM1500 = new conditions from 1500Z
TEMPO = temporary (<1 hour fluctuation)
BECMG = gradual change over ~2 hours
PROB30 = 30% probability — not in first 6 forecast hours
sections.memory_aid: FM = fast change. TEMPO = temporary. BECMG = gradual becoming.

sections.heading: Convective SIGMETs (WST)
sections.body: Convective SIGMETs are issued for severe or extreme convective weather hazards. They are triggered by: severe thunderstorms with surface winds over 50 knots, hail at the surface ≥ 3/4 inch diameter, or tornadoes. Also issued for embedded thunderstorms, lines of thunderstorms, and heavy precipitation affecting 40%+ of a 3,000 sq mile area. Any active Convective SIGMET in your operating area is a hard stop — do not fly.
key_facts: WST = Convective SIGMET
Issued for: surface winds >50 kts, hail ≥3/4 inch, tornadoes, embedded/line thunderstorms
Any active WST in your area = do not fly

sections.heading: Weather Briefing Sources
sections.body: Before every flight, obtain a weather briefing. The FAA-designated source is 1800wxbrief.com or by calling 1-800-WX-BRIEF. A standard briefing covers METARs, TAFs, PIREPs, AIRMETs, SIGMETs, winds aloft, and NOTAMs. Additional resources include aviation.weather.gov, DUATS, and apps like ForeFlight. Always check tfr.faa.gov separately for TFRs.
key_facts: Primary briefing source: 1800wxbrief.com or 1-800-WX-BRIEF
Standard briefing covers: METARs, TAFs, PIREPs, AIRMETs, SIGMETs, winds aloft, NOTAMs
Check tfr.faa.gov separately for TFRs
DUATS also available for pilots with medical certificates

common_mistakes: Confusing TEMPO (temporary, less than 1 hour) with BECMG (gradual, permanent change)
Forgetting that PROB30 is not used in the first 6 hours of a TAF
Checking weather but not checking TFRs separately — they are different products

lessons.title: Atmospheric Stability, Air Masses & Weather Hazards
lessons.intro: Understanding how the atmosphere behaves helps you predict weather hazards. This lesson covers the most tested weather theory topics: stability, fronts, thunderstorms, structural icing, and density altitude.

sections.heading: Stable vs. Unstable Air
sections.body: Air stability determines what kind of weather forms. Stable air resists vertical movement, producing stratiform (layered) clouds, smooth air, continuous precipitation, and poor visibility in haze or smoke. Unstable air encourages vertical movement, producing cumuliform (tall, puffy) clouds, turbulence, showery precipitation, and generally good visibility (except in blowing obstructions).
key_facts: STABLE: stratiform clouds, smooth air, continuous precipitation, poor visibility in haze
UNSTABLE: cumuliform clouds, turbulence, showery precipitation, good visibility
Cool + Dry = Stable
Hot + Humid = Unstable
sections.memory_aid: Stable = Smooth, Stratiform, Steady precipitation, Smoky haze. Unstable = Ugly cumulus, turbUlence.

sections.heading: Thunderstorm Life Cycle
sections.body: A thunderstorm cell progresses through three distinct stages. (1) Cumulus stage: characterized by strong updrafts from the surface to the cloud top — no precipitation yet. (2) Mature stage: most hazardous — both updrafts (up to 3,000 fpm) and downdrafts (up to 6,000 fpm in microbursts), heavy precipitation, lightning, hail. (3) Dissipating stage: updrafts weaken, downdrafts dominate, precipitation tapers off. Transitions between stages are gradual and difficult to detect visually.
key_facts: Stage 1 (Cumulus): updrafts only, no precipitation yet
Stage 2 (Mature): MOST DANGEROUS — updrafts AND downdrafts, lightning, hail
Stage 3 (Dissipating): downdrafts dominate, storm weakens
Updrafts inside a thunderstorm can exceed 3,000 fpm
Microbursts can produce 6,000 fpm downdrafts
sections.memory_aid: Cumulus → Mature → Dissipating. The mature stage will destroy your drone.

sections.heading: Structural Icing
sections.body: Two conditions must exist simultaneously for structural icing to occur: (1) the aircraft must be flying through visible moisture (rain, cloud droplets), AND (2) the temperature at the point of contact must be at or below 0°C. Note: aerodynamic cooling can lower the airfoil surface temperature to 0°C even when ambient air is a few degrees above freezing. If your drone flies into freezing rain or visible clouds in cold conditions, icing is a real threat.
key_facts: Condition 1: Visible moisture (rain or cloud droplets present)
Condition 2: Temperature at impact point ≤ 0°C
BOTH conditions must exist simultaneously
Aerodynamic cooling can cause icing even if ambient temp is slightly above 0°C

sections.heading: Low-Level Wind Shear
sections.body: Wind shear is a sudden, drastic change in wind speed or direction over a very small area. Low-level wind shear is especially hazardous because it occurs close to the ground where recovery options are limited. It is commonly associated with: passing frontal systems, thunderstorms, temperature inversions, and strong upper-level winds greater than 25 knots.
key_facts: Sudden, drastic wind change over a small area
Associated with: fronts, thunderstorms, temperature inversions, upper winds >25 kts
Causes violent updrafts, downdrafts, and abrupt directional changes
Especially dangerous near the surface

sections.heading: Density Altitude and Performance
sections.body: Density altitude is a measure of air density expressed as an equivalent altitude. High density altitude means thin air — fewer air molecules — which reduces propeller efficiency and overall UAS performance. The four factors that increase density altitude (and reduce performance) are: High elevation, High temperature, High humidity, and Heavy weight of the aircraft. Standard atmosphere (for reference): 29.92 in Hg, 59°F (15°C) at sea level.
key_facts: High density altitude = thin air = degraded performance
The 4 H's: Heavy, Hot, High altitude, Humid
Humid air is LESS dense than dry air (water vapor is lighter than N2/O2)
Standard atmosphere: 29.92 in Hg, 59°F (15°C), sea level
Microbursts: 1-2 mile diameter, downdrafts to 6,000 fpm, last 5-15 minutes
sections.memory_aid: The 4 H's kill performance: Heavy, Hot, High, Humid.

common_mistakes: Thinking that good visibility always means stable air — good visibility is actually a sign of UNSTABLE air
Forgetting that BOTH icing conditions must exist at the same time
Thinking humidity improves performance — humid air is actually less dense than dry air

modules.name: Operations
modules.description: The heaviest section of the exam. Covers everything that happens during an actual operation — decision-making, crew coordination, physiological fitness, risk management, and safe procedures.

lessons.title: Aeronautical Decision Making (ADM) & Risk Management
lessons.intro: Human error causes the majority of aviation accidents. ADM is the FAA's systematic approach to making safer decisions in the air. This is one of the most consistently tested topic areas across all exam versions.

sections.heading: The 3P Model — Perceive, Process, Perform
sections.body: The 3P model is the FAA's primary ADM framework for UAS pilots. It is a simple loop applied during all phases of flight: Perceive — gather the current set of circumstances (weather, airspace, equipment, crew fitness). Process — evaluate how those circumstances affect flight safety. Perform — choose and execute the best course of action. The loop repeats continuously throughout the operation.
key_facts: Perceive: gather situational data
Process: evaluate safety impact
Perform: execute best course of action
Applied continuously — before, during, and after flight

sections.heading: PAVE Checklist — Risk Categories
sections.body: PAVE divides flight risk into four categories to ensure nothing is overlooked during preflight planning. P = Pilot-in-Command (use IMSAFE to assess personal fitness). A = Aircraft (is it airworthy? right tool for the job?). V = enVironment (weather, airspace, terrain, TFRs). E = External pressures (deadline, client expectations, desire to impress — the most dangerous category). External pressure management is the single most important key to risk management because it is the one factor that can cause a pilot to ignore all others.
key_facts: P = Pilot (IMSAFE self-assessment)
A = Aircraft (airworthy? suitable for mission?)
V = enVironment (weather, airspace, terrain, TFRs)
E = External pressures — most dangerous, can override all others
External pressure: deadline, desire to impress, pride, goal-completion
sections.memory_aid: PAVE the way before you fly. External pressure = the silent killer.

sections.heading: IMSAFE — Personal Fitness Checklist
sections.body: Before every flight, every crew member (PIC, person manipulating controls, visual observer) should run through IMSAFE. I = Illness. M = Medication. S = Stress. A = Alcohol (8 hours, 0.04 BAC). F = Fatigue. E = Emotion. If any answer is concerning, do not fly. Unlike manned aviation, there is no FAA medical certificate to catch problems — it's entirely on the pilot to self-certify.
key_facts: I = Illness — am I sick?
M = Medication — does it affect judgment or cause drowsiness?
S = Stress — is it affecting concentration?
A = Alcohol — within 8 hours? BAC ≥ 0.04%?
F = Fatigue — adequately rested?
E = Emotion — upset or distracted?
For new medications: wait 48 hours after first dose before flying
sections.memory_aid: I'M SAFE to fly — or I'm not.

sections.heading: The 5 Hazardous Attitudes and Their Antidotes
sections.body: Studies identified five specific mental attitudes that lead to poor decisions. Each has a recognized antidote that pilots should memorize and use to counter the thought when it appears.
key_facts: Anti-authority: 'Don't tell me what to do.' Antidote: 'Follow the rules — they are usually right.'
Impulsivity: 'Do something quickly!' Antidote: 'Not so fast — think first.'
Invulnerability: 'It won't happen to me.' Antidote: 'It could happen to me.'
Macho: 'I can do it — watch me.' Antidote: 'Taking chances is foolish.'
Resignation: 'What's the use?' Antidote: 'I'm not helpless — I can make a difference.'
sections.memory_aid: AIMMR: Anti-authority, Impulsivity, Macho, invulnerability (spelled with a i), Resignation.

sections.heading: Crew Resource Management (CRM)
sections.body: CRM is the effective use of all available resources — human, hardware, and information — to make safe decisions and complete operations successfully. For UAS, CRM applies to all phases: preflight (who checks weather and NOTAMs? who charges batteries? who briefs the crew?), in-flight (who monitors airspace? who tracks battery life?), and post-flight (logging, debrief, maintenance). CRM is not just for multi-person crews — it applies to single-pilot operations as well (called Single-Pilot Resource Management or SRM).
key_facts: CRM = effective use of human resources, hardware, and information
Applies to ALL phases: preflight, in-flight, post-flight
Includes remote PIC, person manipulating controls, visual observers, and support crew
SRM = CRM principles applied to single-pilot operations

sections.heading: Situational Awareness
sections.body: Situational awareness is the accurate perception and understanding of all factors affecting safety. Fatigue, stress, and workload can erode it. When a pilot fixates on one issue and loses awareness of the broader picture, accidents happen. The key is to stay ahead of the operation — anticipate what will change, not just react to what has.
key_facts: Accurate perception and understanding of all factors affecting safety
Fatigue and stress are the primary eroding factors
Fixation on a single item = common cause of accidents
Stay ahead of the aircraft/operation — anticipate, don't just react

summary_table.value: Illness, Medication, Stress, Alcohol, Fatigue, Emotion

common_mistakes: Confusing CRM (applies to all phases) with flight-only decision-making
Forgetting the 5 hazardous attitude names or their antidotes
Not recognizing that external pressure is the MOST dangerous PAVE factor

lessons.title: Physiological Factors — Fitness to Fly
lessons.intro: Your physical and mental state directly affects your ability to safely operate a UAS. Part 107 has no medical certificate requirement — which means the pilot bears full responsibility for self-assessing fitness before every flight.

sections.heading: Alcohol — The Numbers
sections.body: The alcohol rules for Part 107 are more restrictive than typical state DUI laws. No person may act as remote PIC, person manipulating the controls, or visual observer if they: consumed alcohol within the preceding 8 hours, are under the influence of alcohol, or have a BAC of 0.04% or greater. Note that 0.04% is half the typical 0.08% DUI threshold. Even a hangover is an impaired state — alcohol can remain in the body for over 16 hours.
key_facts: 8-hour 'bottle to throttle' rule
BAC limit: 0.04% (half the standard DUI threshold)
Applies to: remote PIC, person manipulating controls, AND visual observer
Hangover = still impaired — alcohol remains in body 16+ hours
Even small amounts impair judgment, coordination, and vision

sections.heading: Medications
sections.body: No CFR regulation specifically prohibits medications by name — but Part 107 and Part 91 prohibit acting as crew when taking anything that affects your faculties contrary to safety. The most commonly implicated drugs are antihistamines (e.g., Benadryl/diphenhydramine) — they cause drowsiness and have a prolonged half-life. For any new medication (OTC or prescribed), wait at least 48 hours after the first dose before flying to confirm you do not have adverse side effects.
key_facts: No specific medications listed by name in the FARs
Prohibition: anything that affects faculties contrary to safety
Common problem drugs: antihistamines (Benadryl), decongestants
New medication rule: wait 48 hours after first dose before flying

sections.heading: Fatigue
sections.body: Fatigue causes degraded attention and concentration, impaired coordination, and reduced ability to communicate and make decisions. It is insidious because pilots often don't recognize how impaired they are until serious errors occur. Acute fatigue (short-term, from sleep loss or exertion) can be resolved with rest. Chronic fatigue (long-term, from sustained high stress) requires medical attention. If suffering from acute fatigue, do not fly. No amount of training or experience overcomes it during a flight.
key_facts: Causes: degraded attention, impaired coordination, poor decisions
Danger: pilots often don't recognize their own fatigue
Acute fatigue: fixed by rest and sleep
Chronic fatigue: requires physician treatment
Do not fly if fatigued — no skill compensates for it

sections.heading: Stress
sections.body: A moderate amount of stress is normal and helpful — it prevents complacency. But performance peaks and then falls sharply as stress exceeds a person's coping capacity. Chronic stress (financial, personal, job) accumulates and impairs judgment even when the pilot thinks they are functioning normally. Stress is not listed among the medical conditions requiring grounding, so it's on the pilot to recognize and manage it.
key_facts: Some stress improves alertness — too much degrades performance
Performance curve: increases → peaks → falls sharply with overload
Acute stress (short-term): fight-or-flight response — manageable
Chronic stress (long-term): requires active management or medical help
Stress is NOT a listed medical condition — pilot must self-regulate

sections.heading: Hyperventilation
sections.body: Hyperventilation is excessive breathing that causes an abnormal loss of CO2 from the blood. It often occurs when a pilot becomes anxious or encounters an unexpected stressful situation. Symptoms include: visual impairment, lightheadedness, tingling sensations, muscle spasms, and possible unconsciousness. Treatment: breathe normally, breathe into a paper bag, or talk aloud to restore CO2 levels. Do NOT increase breathing rate — that worsens the condition.
key_facts: Caused by excessive breathing — too much CO2 exhaled
Symptoms: visual impairment, dizziness, tingling, possible unconsciousness
Treatment: breathe normally, breathe into a paper bag, or talk aloud
Do NOT breathe faster — worsens the condition

sections.heading: Dehydration and Night Vision
sections.body: Dehydration's first effect is fatigue, making top mental and physical performance difficult. Causes include heat, wind, humidity, and diuretic beverages (coffee, alcohol, energy drinks). Drink 2–4 quarts of water per day. For night operations: it takes approximately 30 minutes for eyes to fully adapt to darkness. Exposure to bright lights (phone screen, work light) resets this clock. Use red lights when possible to preserve night vision.
key_facts: First sign of dehydration: fatigue
Other symptoms: headache, cramps, sleepiness, dizziness
Diuretics to avoid: coffee, alcohol, caffeinated drinks
Full dark adaptation: approximately 30 minutes
Bright light exposure resets dark adaptation

summary_table.value: Slow breathing, paper bag, talk aloud

common_mistakes: Thinking alcohol/drug rules only apply to the person actually flying the drone — they apply to all crew
Thinking you can power through fatigue with caffeine or effort during flight
Treating hyperventilation by breathing faster — this worsens it

lessons.title: Preflight, Emergency Procedures & Maintenance
lessons.intro: Safe operations start before takeoff and continue after landing. This lesson covers the required preflight actions, how to handle emergencies, and what to do about maintenance and batteries.

sections.heading: Preflight Familiarization Requirements (§107.49)
sections.body: Before each flight, the remote PIC must be familiar with: (1) airspace classification and ATC requirements for the planned operation, (2) local weather conditions, (3) NOTAMs relevant to the area, (4) known traffic and known hazards in the operating area, and (5) any other information necessary for safe operations. This is not optional — it is a regulatory requirement.
key_facts: Required: airspace check, weather check, NOTAM check, traffic/hazard awareness
Required by §107.49 before every flight
No waiver available for this requirement

sections.heading: Preflight Inspection
sections.body: The remote PIC must inspect the UAS before every flight to ensure it is in a safe operating condition. If any irregularities are found, they must be corrected BEFORE flight. A cracked propeller, damaged motor, low battery, or loose component must be fixed first — not logged and deferred. Some manufacturers provide a preflight checklist. If yours does not, you must develop one yourself.
key_facts: Inspect before every flight — required by §107.49
Any irregularity found must be corrected BEFORE flight — not after
If no manufacturer checklist exists, develop your own
Remote PIC bears responsibility — not the manufacturer, not the FAA
sections.example: You find a hairline crack in one propeller blade during preflight. You must replace the propeller before flying. Flying with a cracked prop risks in-flight failure, loss of control, and potential injury to bystanders.

sections.heading: Crew Briefing
sections.body: When using a crew — visual observer, person manipulating controls, or other support staff — all crew members must be briefed on the planned operation and emergency procedures before the first takeoff. The briefing should cover: the mission profile, operating area, airspace, communication protocols, emergency procedures (lost link, battery fire, flyaway), and each person's role.
key_facts: All crew must be briefed before flight
Briefing must include: mission plan AND emergency procedures
Applies to visual observers and any non-certificated persons on the controls

sections.heading: Lost Link Procedures
sections.body: A lost link occurs when the command-and-control (C2) link between the control station and the aircraft is broken. The aircraft typically follows a pre-programmed contingency behavior (e.g., hover, return to home, or land in place). Before each flight, the remote PIC should verify the lost link behavior is configured appropriately for the environment — a 'return to home' over open fields may not be safe over a crowd. The lost link procedure should be part of the crew briefing.
key_facts: Lost link = C2 link failure between control station and aircraft
Pre-program a contingency behavior before flight
Verify lost link behavior is appropriate for the specific environment
Include in crew briefing

sections.heading: Lithium Battery Safety and Thermal Runaway
sections.body: Lithium polymer (LiPo) batteries power most small UAS. They are energy-dense but sensitive to damage and overcharging. Thermal runaway is a chain reaction where the battery self-heats and releases its stored energy uncontrollably — potentially causing fire or explosion. Warning signs: swelling/puffing, unusual heat during charging, or reduced capacity. Always follow manufacturer recommendations for charging, storage, and replacement. Never charge a damaged or swollen battery. Charge in a safe location where a fire would not spread.
key_facts: Thermal runaway = self-heating chain reaction → fire/explosion
Warning signs: swelling, excessive heat during charging, reduced capacity
Always follow manufacturer recommendations
Never charge a damaged or swollen battery
Charge in a fire-safe location (LiPo bag or fireproof container)

sections.heading: Maintenance
sections.body: While the FAA does not certify small UAS components, Part 107 places maintenance responsibility on the remote PIC. If a manufacturer provides a maintenance schedule, follow it. If no schedule is provided, the remote PIC must establish one. Understanding the typical failure modes for your aircraft helps you prevent crashes rather than investigate them after the fact. Document maintenance actions.
key_facts: Remote PIC responsible for aircraft maintenance
If manufacturer provides schedule: follow it
If no schedule exists: establish your own
FAA delegated inspection to the remote PIC — FAA cannot track all UAS models

summary_table.value: Must correct before flight — no deferral
Remote PIC — establish schedule if none provided

common_mistakes: Thinking a small defect can be flown on and fixed after — any unsafe condition must be corrected before flight
Forgetting the crew briefing must include emergency procedures, not just mission details
Charging LiPo batteries indoors near flammable materials
